title: Peanut Chicken Salad

subs.name: canned tuna (2 cans, drained) — no poaching needed, equally high in protein
spinach or finely sliced capsicum for a softer salad base
tahini or almond butter — use the same quantity in the dressing; changes allergen profile
tahini or almond butter — use the same quantity in the dressing; changes allergen profile

steps.text: Place the chicken breast in a saucepan and cover with cold water. Add a pinch of salt and bring to a gentle boil. Reduce to a very low simmer and poach for 15-17 minutes until the chicken is cooked through — it should be firm but not rubbery when pressed. Do not boil rapidly or the breast will tighten and turn dry.
Transfer the chicken to a board and let it rest for 5 minutes. Use two forks or your fingers to shred it into long, thin pieces along the grain.

While the chicken poaches, whisk together peanut butter, lemon juice, grated ginger, minced garlic, green chilli and honey into a smooth dressing. Thin with 1-2 tbsp of the hot poaching liquid until it pours easily. Taste for salt.

Toss the shredded cabbage, julienned cucumber and spring onion together in a large bowl. Add the shredded chicken and pour the peanut dressing over everything. Toss thoroughly so the dressing coats every strand of chicken and vegetable.

Scatter the crushed roasted peanuts on top and finish with coriander leaves. Serve immediately for the crunchiest texture, or refrigerate for up to 2 days — add the peanuts just before eating so they stay crisp.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chicken → canned tuna (2 cans, drained) — no poaching needed, equally high in protein; peanut → tahini or almond butter — use the same quantity in the dressing; changes allergen profile; cabbage → spinach or finely sliced capsicum for a softer salad base
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Roasted groundnuts (moongphali) are sold at every Indian kirana for a fraction of the price of imported peanut butter — use 3 tbsp crushed groundnuts blended with a splash of oil to make a quick peanut paste that works identically in the dressing. Poach the chicken on any stovetop in a simple saucepan; the technique needs no special equipment.
